title: Modern Outdoor Patio Table
summary: A pressure-treated patio table with laminated 6x6 legs — two 6x6s glued face to face per leg, under a 1x6 plank top with mitered corner supports.
description:
This table's legs are the distinctive detail: each leg is two 6x6 timbers glued face to face and clamped overnight, producing an oversized 11" x 5 1/2" post that reads as genuinely substantial furniture rather than typical deck lumber. Glue-up needs real clamps or, failing that, enough dead weight stacked on top overnight — this joint is 100% glue, so clamping pressure across the whole glued face is what makes or breaks it.

**Use pressure-treated-rated screws and glue throughout, not standard interior pocket-hole screws** — the plan calls this out directly, since standard screws will rust in pressure-treated lumber and outdoor exposure. If your screws are longer than the pocket-hole depth calls for, take care not to drive them through the far side of the board.

Build the top's apron frame first, add corner supports at each 45° corner (drilled straight down the long side so the pilot hole exits through the middle of the mitered cut), then build the tabletop planks separately and flip them onto the apron frame once both are done. **Attaching the laminated legs is a two-person, heavy-lifting step** — mark leg position 18" in from each side on the underside supports, pocket-hole three faces of each leg (skipping the face against the 2x4 support), and pre-drill the 2x4 supports before driving the final 3" screws. Flipping the finished table right-side up at the end genuinely needs several people; this is a heavy table by design.
timeLabel: 8–12 hrs across a weekend, plus overnight glue cure

cutList.note: Each leg is two 6x6s glued face to face and clamped overnight.
Spaced at 19" intervals inside the apron.
Short point measurement, both ends cut at 45° for corners.
Verify actual board length before cutting — boards can run longer than nominal.

steps.title: Laminate the legs
steps.body: Glue one 6x6 face to another to make each leg. Clamp overnight, or weigh down heavily if you lack large enough clamps. Once cured, sand smooth.

steps.title: Build the apron frame
steps.body: Using pocket hole screws, attach the apron long sides to the apron ends with pressure-treated-rated 1-1/4" screws and outdoor wood glue. Do not use standard interior pocket-hole screws — they will rust.

steps.title: Add the center and corner supports
steps.body: Install the center supports at 19" intervals with glue and 1-1/4" screws. Drill straight down the long side of the corner supports so the pilot hole exits through the middle of the 45° cut, then attach all four corners with glue and 1-1/4" screws.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Verify your 1x6 boards' actual length before cutting. Drill 4–5 pocket holes in each board (except the last) and attach edge to edge with 1-1/4" screws and glue, clamping to pull uneven boards together. Flip top-side up and sand out any unevenness.

steps.title: Attach the top to the apron
steps.body: Flip the tabletop back over. Apply glue to the top of the apron structure, flip it down onto the tabletop, and screw together from above.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: While still upside down, mark 18" from each side on the outside 2x4 supports for the leg positions. Pocket-hole the three faces of each leg that won't touch the 2x4 support. Glue and attach with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, then pre-drill the 2x4 support and drive 3" screws through into each leg (about 8 per leg). This is a good time to pre-finish the inside faces of the legs, since there's only 1" of clearance once assembled.

steps.title: Flip and finish
steps.body: With help from several people, flip the table right-side up — it's heavy and awkward for one person. Sand and apply your finish.

unresolvedImages.alt: Outdoor patio table with laminated 6x6 legs and a plank top
